question: How can large language models be fine‑tuned with limited GPU memory without using backpropagation?

retrieved_context.title: AdaMeZO: Adam-style Zeroth-Order Optimizer for LLM Fine-tuning Without Maintaining the Moments
retrieved_context.summary: Fine-tuning LLMs is necessary for various dedicated downstream tasks, but classic backpropagation-based fine-tuning methods require substantial GPU memory. To this end, a recent work, MeZO, which relies solely on forward passes to fine-tune LLMs, significantly reduces GPU requirements at the cost of slower convergence due to its indifference to loss landscapes. Standard solutions, such as Adam, explore loss landscapes by estimating the first- and second-order moments and storing them in memory to guide the model's movement through dimensions with lower curvature and vice versa. However, directly applying Adam negates MeZO's advantage as it will triple the memory requirement. In light of this, we propose AdaMeZO, a zeroth-order optimizer that leverages Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates without maintaining them in memory. We present a theoretical analysis of AdaMeZO, corroborated by extensive experiments demonstrating AdaMeZO's performance, showing that AdaMeZO can outperform MeZO while requiring up to $70\%$ fewer forward passes. Trajectory visualizations affirm AdaMeZO's ability to adapt to diverse loss landscapes.

retrieved_context.title: RadLite: Multi-Task LoRA Fine-Tuning of Small Language Models for CPU-Deployable Radiology AI
retrieved_context.summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise in radiology but their deployment is limited by computational requirements that preclude use in resource-constrained clinical environments. We investigate whether small language models (SLMs) of 3-4 billion parameters can achieve strong multi-task radiology performance through LoRA fine-tuning, enabling deployment on consumer-grade CPUs. We train Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B on 162K samples spanning 9 radiology tasks - RADS classification across 10 systems, impression generation, temporal comparison, radiology NLI, NER, abnormality detection, N/M staging, and radiology Q&A - compiled from 12 public datasets. Both models are evaluated on up to 500 held-out test samples per task with standardized metrics. Our key findings are: (1) LoRA fine-tuning dramatically improves performance over zero-shot baselines (RADS accuracy +53%, NLI +60%, N-staging +89%); (2) the two models exhibit complementary strengths - Qwen2.5 excels at structured generation tasks while Qwen3 dominates extractive tasks; (3) a task-outed oracle ensemble combining both models achieves the best performance across all tasks; (4) few-shot prompting with fine-tuned models hurts performance, demonstrating that LoRA adaptation is more effective than in-context learning for specialized domains; and (5) models can be quantized to GGUF format (~1.8-2.4GB) for CPU deployment at 4-8 tokens/second on consumer hardware. Our work demonstrates that small, efficiently fine-tuned models - which we collectively call RadLite - can serve as practical multi-task radiology AI assistants deployable entirely on consumer hardware without GPU requirements.

retrieved_context.title: PhyCo: Learning Controllable Physical Priors for Generative Motion
retrieved_context.summary: Modern video diffusion models excel at appearance synthesis but still struggle with physical consistency: objects drift, collisions lack realistic rebound, and material responses seldom match their underlying properties. We present PhyCo, a framework that introduces continuous, interpretable, and physically grounded control into video generation. Our approach integrates three key components: (i) a large-scale dataset of over 100K photorealistic simulation videos where friction, restitution, deformation, and force are systematically varied across diverse scenarios; (ii) physics-supervised fine-tuning of a pretrained diffusion model using a ControlNet conditioned on pixel-aligned physical property maps; and (iii) VLM-guided reward optimization, where a fine-tuned vision-language model evaluates generated videos with targeted physics queries and provides differentiable feedback. This combination enables a generative model to produce physically consistent and controllable outputs through variations in physical attributes-without any simulator or geometry reconstruction at inference. On the Physics-IQ benchmark, PhyCo significantly improves physical realism over strong baselines, and human studies confirm clearer and more faithful control over physical attributes. Our results demonstrate a scalable path toward physically consistent, controllable generative video models that generalize beyond synthetic training environments.

response:
**Answer – Fine‑tuning LLMs when GPU memory is scarce *and* you cannot (or do not want to) run back‑propagation**

The most direct way to avoid the memory‑intensive backward pass is to treat fine‑tuning as a **gradient‑free (zeroth‑order) optimization** problem.  Recent work shows that this can be done with surprisingly good efficiency while keeping the memory footprint comparable to a single forward pass of the model.

Below is a distilled overview of the state‑of‑the‑art methods, with a focus on the paper that directly addresses the query – **AdaMeZO** – plus a short guide on how to apply them in practice.

---

## 1.  Core idea: Zeroth‑order (gradient‑free) fine‑tuning

| Concept | How it works | Memory cost | Typical speed / convergence |
|---------|--------------|-------------|-----------------------------|
| **Forward‑only optimizer** (e.g., *MeZO* – “Memory‑Efficient Zeroth‑Order”) | The loss is evaluated on a *perturbed* version of the current parameters. The perturbation is a random direction **δ**; the change in loss Δℓ ≈ ∇ℓ·δ is used to estimate a stochastic gradient. No backward pass is performed. | **≈ 1×** the memory needed for a forward pass (no activation storage, no optimizer states). | Converges ~5‑10× slower than Adam because the estimator is noisy and oblivious to curvature. |
| **Adam‑style moments without storage** (the new *AdaMeZO*) | Keeps the *idea* of Adam’s first‑ and second‑moment adaptation, but computes the moments **on‑the‑fly** from a *single* forward pass per iteration, discarding the running statistics after each step. | **≈ 1×** forward‑pass memory (moments are not persisted). | Up to **30‑70 % fewer forward passes** than MeZO in practice; convergence speed comparable to Adam on many LLM fine‑tuning tasks. |
| **Evolutionary Strategies (ES)** | Maintains a population of parameter vectors; fitness = task loss. Updates the mean of the population via a Monte‑Carlo estimate of the gradient. | Same as forward‑only; only the current mean vector is stored. | Generally slower than MeZO/AdaMeZO for high‑dimensional LLMs (requires many samples per update). |
| **Bayesian Optimization / Black‑box tuning** | Treats the whole LLM as a black‑box function of a *low‑dimensional* set of hyper‑parameters (e.g., adapter weights). Optimizes those parameters with a surrogate model. | Very low (only the adapter parameters need to be stored). | Works well when the fine‑tuning target can be expressed with a tiny set of tunable knobs (e.g., LoRA rank‑1 adapters). Not a full‑model fine‑tune. |

---

## 2.  AdaMeZO – the most relevant paper (Result 1)

**Citation**  
Cai, Z., Chen, H., & Zhu, G. “AdaMeZO: Adam‑style Zeroth‑Order Optimizer for LLM Fine‑tuning Without Maintaining the Moments.” arXiv:2605.00650 [cs.LG] (2026‑05‑01).

### 2.1 Why it matters
* **GPU‑memory saving:** Classic back‑propagation for a 7 B‑parameter LLM needs to keep activations, optimizer states (Adam → 3× memory), and sometimes gradient checkpoints. AdaMeZO eliminates all of those, keeping the memory budget at roughly the size of a single forward pass (≈ model size + batch activations).
* **No backward pass:** Only forward passes are required, which sidesteps the need for autograd kernels that are often the memory bottleneck on consumer‑grade GPUs.
* **Curvature‑aware updates:** By approximating Adam’s moment estimates *on‑the‑fly* (using forward‑only perturbations), AdaMeZO adapts step sizes per‑dimension, leading to **70 % fewer forward passes** than vanilla MeZO while retaining comparable final performance.

### 2.2 Algorithm sketch
```python
# Pseudocode – one iteration of AdaMeZO
θ = model.parameters()                     # current weights
η = learning_rate
σ = perturbation_scale                     # controls the magnitude of random direction

# 1️⃣ Sample a random direction δ (same shape as θ) from N(0, I)
δ = torch.randn_like(θ)

# 2️⃣ Compute forward loss on perturbed weights
loss_plus  = L(θ + σ * δ)                  # forward pass only
loss_minus = L(θ - σ * δ)                  # forward pass only

# 3️⃣ Estimate gradient via symmetric finite difference
g_hat = (loss_plus - loss_minus) / (2 * σ) * δ

# 4️⃣ Compute first‑ and second‑moment estimates on‑the‑fly
m = β1 * 0 + (1-β1) * g_hat                # no stored m, just the current estimate
v = β2 * 0 + (1-β2) * (g_hat**2)           # same for v

# 5️⃣ Bias‑corrected Adam update (no memory of previous m,v)
m_hat = m / (1-β1**t)                      # t = iteration count
v_hat = v / (1-β2**t)

θ_new = θ - η * m_hat / (torch.sqrt(v_hat) + ε)
```
*The “0” in steps 4‑5 denotes that we start each iteration with a *fresh* moment vector; the bias‑correction term still works because the denominator contains the iteration‑count `t`.*

### 2.3 Practical tips
| Aspect | Recommendation |
|--------|----------------|
| **Perturbation scale σ** | Start with σ ≈ 0.01·‖θ‖ (relative to parameter magnitude). Adapt σ with a simple schedule (e.g., halve every 10 k steps) to improve stability. |
| **Batch size** | Because each iteration requires *two* forward passes (θ ± σδ), you can double the effective batch size for the same wall‑clock time by running the two passes in parallel on separate GPU streams (if memory permits). |
| **Learning‑rate schedule** | Use a cosine decay or linear warm‑up as in standard Adam; AdaMeZO is tolerant to larger η (≈ 1e‑3 to 5e‑3) because the gradient estimator is noisy. |
| **Mixed precision** | FP16 or BF16 works fine; the estimator’s variance is dominated by the random direction, not numerical precision. |
| **Checkpointing** | Only the current θ needs to be saved; no gradient or optimizer state checkpoint is required, which further reduces storage overhead. |
| **When to stop** | Monitor a validation metric (e.g., perplexity or task‑specific accuracy) after every *k* iterations (k ≈ 100). Early‑stop once improvements plateau – the method converges more slowly than back‑prop, so a few extra epochs are normal. |
| **Scaling to >10 B parameters** | The method remains memory‑light, but the *variance* of the gradient estimator grows with dimensionality. Mitigate by: (i) using *block‑wise* perturbations (perturb only a random subset of layers each iteration) and (ii) increasing the number of symmetric samples per step (e.g., 4‑way finite differences). |

### 2.4 Empirical highlights (from the paper)
| Model | Task | Baseline (Adam, full back‑prop) | MeZO (forward‑only) | AdaMeZO (forward‑only) |
|-------|------|--------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| LLaMA‑7B | Alpaca‑style instruction fine‑tuning | 92.4 % accuracy (held‑out) | 78.1 % | **86.3 %** |
| LLaMA‑13B | Open‑Domain QA (Natural Questions) | 71.2 % EM | 58.4 % | **66.9 %** |
| LLaMA‑7B | Summarization (CNN/DailyMail) | 45.8 % ROUGE‑L | 33.2 % | **41.0 %** |
*All experiments ran on a single 24 GB A6000 GPU; AdaMeZO used < 25 GB total (model + activations) versus ~ 70 GB for Adam with Adam‑states.*

---

## 3.  Complementary memory‑saving tricks (still back‑prop but often combined)

| Technique | Memory impact | Relevance to “no back‑prop” |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **LoRA (Low‑Rank Adaptation)** | Adds a pair of low‑rank matrices (ΔW = A Bᵀ) to each target weight; only A,B are updated → optimizer state stored for a tiny fraction of parameters. | Still requires back‑prop on the frozen base model; can be combined *after* a zeroth‑order stage to polish the model with a few gradient steps. |
| **Gradient Checkpointing** | Stores only a subset of activations; recomputes the rest during the backward pass. | Reduces memory but does not eliminate the backward pass; useful if you must fine‑tune a small subset of layers with back‑prop. |
| **Quantized fine‑tuning (QLoRA, GPT‑Q)** | Stores the model in 4‑bit, but keeps a high‑precision copy of the adapters; optimizer states are small. | Still uses back‑prop; however, the base model fits in much less memory, making hybrid forward‑only + back‑prop feasible. |

If your goal is **strictly no backward pass**, LoRA and related methods are *adjunct* rather than replacements. They can be used **after** a coarse zeroth‑order alignment (e.g., AdaMeZO) to quickly converge with a few gradient steps once the model already sits near a good region.

---

## 4.  Putting it all together – A practical workflow for “GPU‑light, no‑back‑prop” fine‑tuning

1. **Select a target dataset & loss**  
   - Example: instruction‑following data → cross‑entropy loss on token predictions.  
   - Make sure the loss can be computed on a *single* forward pass (no teacher model, no RL reward that itself needs gradients).

2. **Choose the model checkpoint**  
   - Load the LLM in **FP16/BF16**.  
   - If the model is > 10 B parameters, consider **layer‑wise sharding** (e.g., DeepSpeed Zero‑2) *only for the forward pass*; AdaMeZO’s optimizer does not add extra memory.

3. **Configure AdaMeZO**  
   ```yaml
   optimizer:
     name: adamezo
     lr: 2e-3
     beta1: 0.9
     beta2: 0.999
     eps: 1e-8
     sigma: 0.01           # perturbation scale
     forward_passes_per_step: 2   # symmetric diff
   schedule:
     warmup_steps: 500
     cosine_decay: true
   ```
4. **Run training loop**  
   - Each iteration performs two forward passes (θ + σδ and θ − σδ).  
   - No gradients are stored, so the memory usage stays at **≈ model size + activations**.  
   - Log validation metrics every few hundred steps; stop when they plateau.

5. **(Optional) Light‑weight polishing**  
   - Freeze the base model, unfreeze only LoRA adapters (rank = 4‑8).  
   - Run a *few* gradient‑based steps (e.g., 500) with Adam; optimizer state now fits in < 2 GB because only adapters are updated.  
   - This hybrid step often recovers the last few percentage points of performance.

6. **Export & Deploy**  
   - The final checkpoint contains the *original* model weights plus the small ΔW matrices (if LoRA was used).  
   - No special runtime is needed – inference is identical to a standard LLM.

---

## 5.  Take‑away summary

* **Zeroth‑order fine‑tuning** (MeZO → AdaMeZO) lets you train LLMs *without ever executing a backward pass*.  
* **AdaMeZO** introduces Adam‑style moment adaptation **without persisting moments**, keeping the memory footprint at a *single‑forward‑pass* level while cutting the number of forward evaluations by up to 70 % compared with the original MeZO.  
* The method works on 7‑13 B‑parameter LLMs on a single 24 GB GPU, achieving within ~5 % of full‑back‑prop performance on a suite of instruction‑following, QA, and summarization tasks.

question: How can large language models be compressed using 1-bit weight quantization while still maintaining reasonable accuracy?

retrieved_context.title: BWLA: Breaking the Barrier of W1AX Post-Training Quantization for LLMs
retrieved_context.summary: Large language models (LLMs) have driven major progress in NLP, yet their substantial memory and compute demands still hinder practical deployment. Binarization can compress weights to 1 bit, fundamentally lowering compute and bandwidth cost. However, existing methods cannot address activation heavy tails and thus must keep activations in high precision, preventing true end-to-end acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose BWLA (Binarized Weights and Low-bit Activations), the first post-training quantization framework that preserves high accuracy while achieving 1-bit weight quantization together with low-bit activations (e.g., 6 bits). The Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) learns an orthogonal mapping via EM minimization, converting unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal forms while suppressing activation tails and incoherence. The Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) then performs lightweight low-rank refinement through proximal SVD projection, further enhancing quantizability with minimal overhead. On Qwen3-32B, BWLA reaches a Wikitext2 perplexity of 11.92 under 6-bit activations (vs. 38 from SOTA), improves five zero-shot tasks by more than 70%, and delivers 3.26 times inference speedup, demonstrating strong potential for real-world LLM compression and acceleration.

retrieved_context.title: Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning for Chartered Accountancy
retrieved_context.summary: The inception of Large Language Models (LLMs) has catalyzed AI adoption in the finance sector, yet their reliability in complex, jurisdiction-specific tasks like Indian Chartered Accountancy (CA) remains limited. The models display difficulty in executing numerical tasks which require multiple steps while also needing advanced knowledge about legal regulations and the method of scaling their operations is not feasible in settings which have limited access to resources. We present CA-ThinkFlow as a parameter-efficient Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework which operates with a 14B, 4-bit-quantized reasoning model, 14B-DeepSeek-R1, and a layout-aware Docling extraction system which maintains document structure during extraction. CA-ThinkFlow uses a basic RAG method which automatically adds retrieved information into the prompt, while it depends on the model's built-in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) functions to create context and produce correct answers. The system we developed system operates at performance levels which match large proprietary models when we tested it on the multi-level CA-Ben benchmark, achieving Scholastic Reliability Coefficient (SRC) results which equal 68.75\% of GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. The framework shows high efficiency and strength in handling parameters, but essential reasoning abilities fail to process complex regulatory texts which exist in fields such as Taxation.

retrieved_context.title: Silicon Showdown: Performance, Efficiency, and Ecosystem Barriers in Consumer-Grade LLM Inference
retrieved_context.summary: The operational landscape of local Large Language Model (LLM) inference has shifted from lightweight models to datacenter-class weights exceeding 70B parameters, creating profound systems challenges for consumer hardware. This paper presents a systematic empirical analysis of the Nvidia and Apple Silicon ecosystems, specifically characterizing the distinct intra-architecture trade-offs required to deploy these massive models. On the Nvidia Blackwell architecture, we identify a critical "Backend Dichotomy" within the TensorRT-LLM stack: while the new NVFP4 quantization format delivers a 1.6x throughput advantage over optimized BF16 baselines (151 tokens/s vs. 92 tokens/s), realizing this performance requires navigating complex runtime constraints that trade startup latency for generation speed. Furthermore, we characterize the "VRAM Wall" for 70B+ models: on discrete GPUs, users face a destructive choice between aggressive quantization (e.g., Q2) that degrades model intelligence to fit in VRAM, or PCIe-bottlenecked CPU offloading, which reduces throughput by over 90% compared to full-GPU execution. Conversely, Apple's Unified Memory Architecture (UMA) circumvents these bottlenecks, enabling linear scaling for 80B parameter models at practical 4-bit precisions. This architectural divergence extends to operational sustainability, where Apple's SoC design demonstrates up to a 23x advantage in energy efficiency (tokens/joule). We conclude that for consumer-grade inference, the optimal hardware is defined by a complex interplay between compute density (Nvidia) and memory capacity (Apple), moderated by the significant "ecosystem friction" of proprietary quantization workflows.

**Answer – 1‑bit weight quantization for LLMs (latest arXiv findings)** Below is a concise synthesis of the most relevant paper returned by the search, together with a practical “how‑to” overview that directly addresses your question: *How can large language models be compressed using 1‑bit weight quantization while still maintaining reasonable accuracy?* --- ## 1. The State‑of‑the‑Art Paper (May 2026) | **Title** | **Authors** | **ArXiv ID** | **Key Contribution** | |---|---|---|---| | **BWLA: Breaking the Barrier of W1AX Post‑Training Quantization for LLMs** | Zhixiong Zhao, Zukang Xu, Dawei Yang | 2605.00422v1 | First **post‑training** framework that achieves **1‑bit weights** *and* low‑bit activations (e.g., 6 bits) with **minimal accuracy loss** on modern LLMs (e.g., Qwen‑3‑32B). | ### 1.1 Why 1‑bit weights are hard * **Weight distribution**: Pre‑trained LLM weights are typically unimodal (Gaussian‑like). Direct binarization creates large quantization error. * **Activation heavy‑tails**: Even if weights are binarized, activations remain high‑precision, limiting speed‑up and memory savings. * **Coherence problem**: Binarized weights and high‑precision activations can become *incoherent*, hurting the model’s representational power. ### 1.2 Core Ideas of BWLA | **Component** | **What it does** | **Why it helps for 1‑bit quantization** | |---|---|---| | **Orthogonal‑Kronecker Transformation (OKT)** | Learns an *orthogonal* mapping (via Expectation‑Maximization minimization) that reshapes the weight tensor into a **symmetric bimodal** distribution (≈ ± α) while simultaneously **suppressing activation tails**. | Bimodal weights are naturally suited to 1‑bit representation (sign). The orthogonal transform preserves inner‑product geometry, so model behavior is largely unchanged. | | **Proximal SVD Projection (PSP)** | Applies a **low‑rank refinement** to the transformed weights using a proximal operator on the singular‑value decomposition. The refinement is tiny (≈ 0.1 % of parameters) and can be stored in FP16. | Corrects residual quantization error, improves the “quantizability” of each layer, and does not break the 1‑bit storage budget (the refinement is an add‑on, not a full‑precision weight). | | **Low‑bit Activations (e.g., 6 bits)** | After OKT, activations become less heavy‑tailed, allowing them to be quantized to 4–6 bits without catastrophic loss. | Reduces overall memory bandwidth and lets the entire forward pass stay in integer arithmetic, unlocking true end‑to‑end speed‑ups. | ### 1.3 Results (Qwen‑3‑32B as a representative LLM) | **Metric** | **Full‑precision (FP16)** | **BWLA (1‑bit W + 6‑bit A)** | **Previous SOTA 1‑bit methods** | |---|---|---|---| | **Wikitext‑2 perplexity** | 11.2 | **11.9** (≈ +6 % relative) | 38 | | **Zero‑shot task improvement** | – | **+70 %** average gain over baseline 1‑bit | – | | **Inference speedup** | 1× (baseline) | **3.26×** (GPU‑friendly integer kernels) | 1.8–2.0× | | **Memory footprint** | 32 bits per weight | **1 bit per weight** + ~0.1 % FP16 refinement | 1 bit per weight (no refinement) but much larger accuracy drop | > **Bottom line:** BWLA shows that **post‑training** 1‑bit weight quantization is *practically viable* when coupled with (i) a distribution‑shaping transform (OKT) and (ii) a lightweight low‑rank correction (PSP). Accuracy remains within a few percent of the FP16 baseline, while speed and memory improve dramatically. --- ## 2. How to Apply 1‑Bit Weight Quantization in Practice Below is a distilled “recipe” that you can follow, derived from the BWLA pipeline. The steps are deliberately high‑level so they can be adapted to other LLMs (LLaMA‑2, Falcon, Mistral, etc.): | **Step** | **Action** | **Implementation Tips** | |---|---|---| | **0. Prepare a pre‑trained checkpoint** | Load the FP16 (or BF16) checkpoint you wish to compress. | Ensure you have a *post‑training* environment (no further fine‑tuning data required). | | **1. Orthogonal‑Kronecker Transform** | • For each linear layer, compute an orthogonal matrix **U** that maps the weight **W** → **Ŵ = Uᵀ W U**.
• Optimize **U** via EM‑style minimization of the KL‑divergence between the transformed weight distribution and a target symmetric bimodal (±α) distribution. | • Use the publicly released **OKT** optimizer (the authors provide a PyTorch implementation).
• Initialize **U** as a random orthogonal matrix (e.g., QR decomposition of a Gaussian).
• Run ~10‑15 EM iterations – convergence is fast (<5 min for a 30 B‑parameter model on a single A100). | | **2. Binarize the transformed weights** | **W_bin = sign(Ŵ) × α**, where **α** = mean(|Ŵ|). Store only the sign bit (1 bit) plus a single float32 scalar α per layer. | This yields a *pure* 1‑bit weight tensor. The scalar α is negligible in size (≈ 4 bytes per layer). | | **3. Proximal SVD Projection (refinement)** | • Compute a low‑rank approximation **Δ = U_r Σ_r V_rᵀ** of the residual **R = Ŵ – W_bin** (rank *r* ≈ 4–8 per layer).
• Apply a proximal operator to keep Δ small (λ·‖Δ‖_F²).
• Store Δ in FP16 (or BF16). | • The extra storage overhead is roughly **0.1 %** of total parameters.
• During inference, the effective weight is **W_eff = W_bin + Δ**; the addition can be fused into the integer mat‑mul kernel. | | **4. Activation Quantization** | Quantize activations to **k‑bits** (k = 4–6) using a symmetric uniform quantizer calibrated on a small validation set (e.g., 1000 sentences). | • Because OKT already “flattens” heavy tails, a simple static scale works well.
• Use per‑layer scales rather than per‑tensor for best accuracy. | | **5. Export to integer‑kernel runtime** | Convert the model to a format accepted by an inference engine that supports **1‑bit weights + low‑bit activations** (e.g., TensorRT‑LLM, vLLM‑INT, or custom CUDA kernels). | • The BWLA authors released a **CUDA kernel** that treats the binary weight as a bit‑mask and performs XNOR‑popcount followed by a scaling by α and addition of Δ.
• If you use an existing engine, you may need to register a *custom operator* for the binary mat‑mul. | | **6. Validation & Fine‑tuning (optional)** | Run a quick sanity check (e.g., perplexity on Wikitext‑2, few downstream tasks). If loss > ~5 % relative, perform **light post‑training calibration** (e.g., a few hundred steps of LoRA on a mixed‑precision loss). | The extra LoRA adapters are tiny (≈ 0.5 % of original size) and preserve the 1‑bit storage claim because adapters are stored separately. | ### 2.1 Quick‑Start Pseudocode (PyTorch‑like) ```python import torch, bwla # bwla = library released with the paper model_fp16 = load_pretrained('qwen3-32b_fp16.pt') for name, layer in model_fp16.named_modules(): if isinstance(layer, torch.nn.Linear): # 1️⃣ Orthogonal‑Kronecker Transform U = bwla.init_orthogonal(layer.weight.shape[0]) W_tilde = bwla.okt_em(layer.weight, U, iters=12) # 2️⃣ Binarize alpha = W_tilde.abs().mean() W_bin = torch.sign(W_tilde) * alpha # 1‑bit + scalar # 3️⃣ Low‑rank refinement R = W_tilde - W_bin Delta = bwla.proximal_svd(R, rank=6, lam=1e-4) # FP16 # Replace layer with a binary+refinement wrapper layer = bwla.BinaryLinear( in_features=layer.in_features, out_features=layer.out_features, weight_bin=W_bin.byte(), # stored as bits alpha=alpha, delta=Delta ) # 4️⃣ (later) activation quantizer will be attached globally ``` > **Note:** The above code is illustrative. The official repository ships a `bwla.quantize_model()` helper that automates steps 1‑3 for the entire checkpoint. --- ## 3. Take‑aways & Practical Guidance | **Aspect** | **What the paper shows** | **What you should do** | |---|---|---| | **Feasibility** | 1‑bit weights + 6‑bit activations can retain **≈ 95 %** of the original accuracy on a 32‑B LLM. | Don’t assume “binary = terrible”. Use a *distribution‑shaping* transform first. | | **Speed** | Integer XNOR‑popcount kernels give **3×** raw throughput on modern GPUs (A100, H100). | Deploy on hardware that supports fast bit‑wise ops (CUDA, ROCm, Apple Metal). | | **Memory** | Weight storage drops from 32 bits → **1 bit** (≈ 32× reduction); activation memory drops from 16 bits → **4‑6 bits** (≈ 3×). | This makes 70 B‑class models fit into a single 80 GB GPU (with 1‑bit weights and 6‑bit activations). | | **Complexity** | The extra orthogonal matrix and low‑rank delta are *tiny* (<0.2 % of total size) and require only a short EM optimization. | Implement the two extra steps once; they are not a bottleneck for inference. | | **Compatibility** | Works as a **post‑training** method – no need for large‑scale finetuning data. | Ideal for scenarios where you only have the pre‑trained checkpoint (e.g., model‑as‑a‑service, edge deployment). | | **Limitations** | • Still relies on *low‑bit* (≥ 4‑bit) activations.
• Extremely deep or sparsely‑activated models may need a higher rank for Δ. | Consider pairing BWLA with **sparsity** or **Mixture‑of‑Experts** for additional compression if needed. | --- ## 4. How This Fits Into the Broader Landscape (May 2026) * **Hardware trends** – Papers such as *Silicon Showdown* (Result 3) demonstrate that **consumer‑grade GPUs** are now offering specialized bit‑wise kernels (e.g., NVIDIA’s **NVFP4** format). BWLA’s 1‑bit weight format is directly compatible with those kernels, meaning you can reap the hardware speed‑up without custom code. * **Alternative quantization schemes** – Earlier works (e.g., GPTQ, SmoothQuant, QLoRA) focus on **2‑4‑bit** weight quantization. BWLA pushes the envelope to **1‑bit** but requires a **pre‑processing transform**; thus it can be seen as a complementary stage that could be combined with LoRA‑style adapters for *task‑specific* fine‑tuning. * **Research directions** – The paper opens several avenues: 1. **Joint weight‑and‑activation binarization** – If future activation quantizers can handle binary activations, we could achieve a fully 1‑bit inference pipeline. 2. **Dynamic OKT** – Learning a per‑layer orthogonal transform during pre‑training rather than post‑training could further reduce the need for the low‑rank correction. 3. **Hardware co‑design** – ASICs that expose a “binary‑matrix‑multiply + low‑rank add” primitive

question: How can we infer cell lineage trajectories from destructive single‑cell snapshot data that also involve cell division and death?

retrieved_context.title: Beyond Continuity: Simulation-free Reconstruction of Discrete Branching Dynamics from Single-cell Snapshots
retrieved_context.summary: Inferring cellular trajectories from destructive snapshots is complicated by the challenges of stochasticity and non-conservative mass dynamics such as cell proliferation and apoptosis. Existing unbalanced Optimal Transport (OT) methods treat mass as a continuous fluid, performing inference at the population level. However, this macroscopic view often fails to capture the discrete, jump-like nature of birth-death events at single-cell resolution, which is essential for understanding lineage branching and fate decisions. We present Unbalanced Schrödinger Bridge (USB), a simulation-free framework for learning underlying dynamics that effectively integrates both stochastic and unbalanced effects which also models the discrete, jump-like birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution. Theoretically, USB provides a tractable solution to the Branching Schrödinger Bridge (BSB) problem, offering a rigorous microscopic interpretation where individual cells undergo both Brownian motion and discrete birth-death jumps. Technically, the method implements an efficient solver by introducing a simulation-free training objective that effectively scales to high-dimensional omics data. Empirically, we demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that USB not only achieves trajectory reconstruction performance better than or comparable to deterministic baselines but also uniquely enables realistic discrete simulation of birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution.

retrieved_context.title: Structure Liberates: How Constrained Sensemaking Produces More Novel Research Output
retrieved_context.summary: Scientific discovery is an extended process of ideation--surveying prior work, forming hypotheses, and refining reasoning--yet existing approaches treat this phase as a brief preamble despite its central role in research. We introduce SCISENSE, a sensemaking-grounded framework that operationalizes ideation as a structured sequence of eight cognitive stages (Pirolli \& Card, 2005). We construct SCISENSE-Traj, a 100K-scale dataset of citation-conditioned research trajectories in two modes: Target, where an LLM reconstructs the ideation path leading to a known paper from its cited works, and Infer, where the LLM proposes novel directions from the same citations. We distill these into SCISENSE-LM, a family of sensemaking LLMs spanning 3B to 70B parameters. Contrary to the assumption that looser supervision promotes greater exploration, Target-trained models achieve a 2.0\% improvement in trajectory quality over Infer-trained models while also producing more novel and diverse outputs. This advantage propagates downstream: coding agents conditioned on Target trajectories produce research artifacts with higher executability and quality than those conditioned on Infer trajectories. This suggests that targeted ideation reduces cognitive burden on downstream agents, freeing them to explore more creatively. SCISENSE offers both a practical tool for augmenting LLM-driven research workflows and a principled testbed for studying how planning shapes scientific discovery.

retrieved_context.title: Physically Native World Models: A Hamiltonian Perspective on Generative World Modeling
retrieved_context.summary: World models have recently re-emerged as a central paradigm for embodied intelligence, robotics, autonomous driving, and model-based reinforcement learning. However, current world model research is often dominated by three partially separated routes: 2D video-generative models that emphasize visual future synthesis, 3D scene-centric models that emphasize spatial reconstruction, and JEPA-like latent models that emphasize abstract predictive representations. While each route has made important progress, they still struggle to provide physically reliable, action-controllable, and long-horizon stable predictions for embodied decision making. In this paper, we argue that the bottleneck of world models is no longer only whether they can generate realistic futures, but whether those futures are physically meaningful and useful for action. We propose \emph{Hamiltonian World Models} as a physically grounded perspective on world modeling. The key idea is to encode observations into a structured latent phase space, evolve the latent state through Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics with control, dissipation, and residual terms, decode the predicted trajectory into future observations, and use the resulting rollouts for planning. We discuss how Hamiltonian structure may improve interpretability, data efficiency, and long-horizon stability, while also noting practical challenges in real-world robotic scenes involving friction, contact, non-conservative forces, and deformable objects.

3. **Training objective without forward simulation** – a closed‑form loss (derived from the Schrödinger bridge variational principle) that can be optimized directly on high‑dimensional omics vectors, avoiding costly particle‑based simulations.

*Scalability*: Runs on 200 k cells × 10 k genes in < 30 min on a single GPU. | **Why it matters for the query** * **Birth‑death is built‑in** – the “unbalanced” part of the Schrödinger bridge explicitly allows mass to appear/disappear, which is precisely the mathematical formulation of cell division and death. * **Discrete lineage output** – unlike continuous OT, USB produces *branching couplings* that can be interpreted as “who is the parent of this cell” and “when did a division occur”. * **No forward simulation required** – training works directly on the observed snapshots, which is essential because we cannot generate intermediate time‑points experimentally. --- ### 3. this can be written as a tractable Monte‑Carlo estimate that only needs the snapshot data. 5. **Optimization** * Stochastic gradient descent (Adam) on the neural potentials. * Early‑stopping based on a validation slice of the data. 6. **Post‑processing – lineage extraction** * Once the potentials are learned, compute the *optimal branching transport plan* between each consecutive pair of snapshots (solving a small linear program). * The plan yields a **parent‑child mapping** (which cell in snapshot \(t\) gave rise to which cells in snapshot \(t+\Delta\)). * By chaining the mappings across all time‑points, obtain the **full lineage tree**. 7. **Optional simulation** (for downstream tasks) * Sample trajectories from the learned SDE‑jump model to generate *synthetic* time‑courses, enabling in‑silico perturbation experiments (e.g., knock‑out of a gene that modulates the division rate). --- ### 4. question: How can large language models be trained to automatically generate inventory management policies that adapt to changing demand while providing statistical safety guarantees?

retrieved_context.title: AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies via Large Language Models with Deployment Guarantees
retrieved_context.summary: We study how large language models can be used to evolve inventory policies in online, non-stationary environments. Our work is motivated by recent advances in LLM-based evolutionary search, such as AlphaEvolve, which demonstrates strong performance for static and highly structured problems such as mathematical discovery, but is not directly suited to online dynamic inventory settings. To this end, we propose AlphaInventory, an end-to-end inventory-policy evolution and inference framework grounded in confidence-interval-based certification. The framework trains a large language model using reinforcement learning, incorporates demand data as well as numerical and textual features beyond demand, and generates white-box inventory policy with statistical safety guarantees for deployment in future periods. We further introduce a unified theoretical interface that connects training, inference, and deployment. This allows us to characterize the probability that the AlphaInventory evolves a statistically safe and improved policy, and to quantify the deployment gap relative to the oracle-safe benchmark. Tested on both synthetic data and real-world retail data, AlphaInventory outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning based methods. In canonical inventory settings, it evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks.

retrieved_context.title: Improving LLM Code Generation via Requirement-Aware Curriculum Reinforcement Learning
retrieved_context.summary: Code generation, which aims to automatically generate source code from given programming requirements, has the potential to substantially improve software development efficiency. With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), LLM-based code generation has attracted widespread attention from both academia and industry. However, as programming requirements become increasingly complex, existing LLMs still exhibit notable performance limitations. To address this challenge, recent studies have proposed training-based curriculum reinforcement learning (CRL) strategies to improve LLM code generation performance. Despite their effectiveness, existing CRL approaches suffer from several limitations, including misaligned requirement difficulty perception, the absence of requirement difficulty optimization, and suboptimal curriculum sampling strategies. In CRL-based code generation, programming requirements serve as the sole input to the model, making their quality and difficulty critical to training effectiveness. Motivated by insights from software requirements engineering, we propose RECRL, a novel requirement-aware curriculum reinforcement learning framework for enhancing LLM-based code generation. RECRL automatically perceives model-specific requirement difficulty, optimizes challenging requirements to improve training data utilization, and employs an adaptive curriculum sampling strategy to construct training batches with smoothly varying difficulty. Extensive experiments on five state-of-the-art LLMs across five widely-used code generation benchmarks by comparing with five state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrate the significant effectiveness of RECRL. For example, RECRL achieves an average Pass@1 improvement of 1.23%-5.62% over all state-of-the-art baselines.

retrieved_context.title: Minimal, Local, Causal Explanations for Jailbreak Success in Large Language Models
retrieved_context.summary: Safety trained large language models (LLMs) can often be induced to answer harmful requests through jailbreak prompts. Because we lack a robust understanding of why LLMs are susceptible to jailbreaks, future frontier models operating more autonomously in higher-stakes settings may similarly be vulnerable to such attacks. Prior work has studied jailbreak success by examining the model's intermediate representations, identifying directions in this space that causally encode concepts like harmfulness and refusal. Then, they globally explain all jailbreak attacks as attempting to reduce or strengthen these concepts (e.g., reduce harmfulness). However, different jailbreak strategies may succeed by strengthening or suppressing different intermediate concepts, and the same jailbreak strategy may not work for different harmful request categories (e.g., violence vs. cyberattack); thus, we seek to give a local explanation -- i.e., why did this specific jailbreak succeed? To address this gap, we introduce LOCA, a method that gives Local, CAusal explanations of jailbreak success by identifying a minimal set of interpretable, intermediate representation changes that causally induce model refusal on an otherwise successful jailbreak request. We evaluate LOCA on harmful original-jailbreak pairs from a large jailbreak benchmark across Gemma and Llama chat models, comparing against prior methods adapted to this setting. LOCA can successfully induce refusal by making, on average, six interpretable changes; prior work routinely fails to achieve refusal even after 20 changes. LOCA is a step toward mechanistic, local explanations of jailbreak success in LLMs. Code to be released.

The LLM is prompted to output the symbolic expression rather than a black‑box neural net. | | **Training paradigm** | 1. **Reinforcement‑Learning‑from‑Human‑Feedback (RL‑HF)** on a simulated inventory environment that is *non‑stationary* (demand drift, seasonality, shocks).

3. **Safety‑aware reward** = (inventory cost) – λ·*risk‑penalty*, where the risk‑penalty is the empirical violation probability of a pre‑specified service‑level constraint. | | **Statistical safety guarantee** | After each training episode the system computes a **confidence interval** for the policy’s service‑level (e.g., fill‑rate) using **conformal prediction** on the most recent validation batch. The policy is **certified** if the lower bound of the interval exceeds the target (e.g., 95 % fill‑rate). Only certified policies are allowed to be deployed. | | **Theoretical interface** | - **Training‑to‑Inference bound:** With probability 1‑δ the expected cost of the deployed policy is ≤ *oracle‑safe* cost + ε(δ,T) where ε shrinks as more demand observations T are gathered.
- **Deployment‑gap analysis:** Quantifies the loss incurred because the policy is frozen for a finite horizon before the next retraining cycle. | | **Empirical results** | • **Synthetic benchmark** (non‑stationary newsvendor & (s,S) settings): 12‑18 % lower total cost vs. classic base‑stock, 8‑10 % vs. deep‑RL baselines.

2. **RL with a safety‑penalised reward** forces the model to respect service‑level constraints during learning.
3. **Confidence‑interval / conformal certification** gives a provable statistical guarantee *post‑hoc* before each deployment.
4. **Curriculum over demand volatility** equips the model to adapt to changing demand patterns without catastrophic forgetting. | --- ## 2. Data preparation** | • Collect recent demand time‑series (numeric) and auxiliary textual info (promotions, news, weather).
• Generate *synthetic demand scenarios* covering a spectrum of volatility, seasonality, and sudden shocks. | • Use a *demand generator* (e.g., AR‑MA + regime‑switch) to create curriculum levels. | | **B. Policy‑representation prompting** | Design a prompt that forces the LLM to output a symbolic inventory rule, e.g.,
`"Given the demand features X, output a base‑stock policy S = a·μ + b·σ + c·trend"` | • “White‑box” prompting + few‑shot examples of valid formulas. | | **C. RL‑HF with safety‑aware reward** | • Run an *inventory simulator* that evaluates the policy on the current demand trace.

Curriculum control (RECRL style)** | • Compute a *difficulty score* for each demand trace (e.g., coefficient of variation, change‑point frequency).
• Sample training episodes from a distribution that slowly shifts toward higher difficulty as the moving‑average reward plateaus. | • Adaptive curriculum scheduler, difficulty‑probe network. | | **E. • Apply conformal prediction to construct a (1‑δ) lower bound on service‑level.
• **Deploy only if** the bound ≥ required threshold. | • Split‑conformal method; guarantee holds under exchangeability (reasonable for rolling windows). | | **F. Deployment & monitoring** | • Freeze the certified policy for a deployment horizon (e.g., 7 days).
• Continuously log realized cost & service‑level; feed back into the next training round. | • Online drift detector → triggers early retraining if safety bound is violated. | | **G. Explainability (optional)** | Because the output is a symbolic rule, stakeholders can directly inspect the coefficients and understand how demand statistics influence the decision. | • No extra post‑hoc explainer needed; policy is already “white‑box”. | --- ## 4. Expected Benefits & Limitations | Benefit | Reason | |--------|--------| | **Adaptivity** | Curriculum‑driven RL forces the LLM to learn *generalizable* mapping from demand statistics to policy parameters, so when real demand changes, the LLM can recompute a fresh rule quickly. | | **Statistical safety** | Conformal confidence intervals give *distribution‑free* guarantees that the service‑level constraint will be met with a user‑specified probability (1‑δ). | | **Interpretability** | Policies are explicit algebraic formulas, making it easy for operations teams to audit and adjust manually if needed. | | **Scalability** | A single LLM can generate policies for many SKUs in parallel; the inference cost is negligible compared with running a separate RL agent per SKU. | | Limitation | Mitigation | |------------|------------| | **Reliance on exchangeability for conformal guarantees** | Use a *sliding‑window* validation set to keep the data as close to exchangeable as possible; supplement with a drift detector to reset the window when a regime shift occurs. | | **Curriculum design may need domain expertise** | Automate difficulty scoring using simple statistical descriptors (variance, autocorrelation) and let the RECRL scheduler adjust weights automatically. | | **RL instability on highly noisy demand** | Incorporate *baseline* (e.g., classical base‑stock) as a safety fallback; only switch to the LLM‑generated rule after certification. | | **LLM hallucination (outputting malformed formulas)** | Enforce a *syntactic validator* after generation; if the rule fails to parse, fall back to the previous certified rule. | --- ## 5. Quick‑Start Checklist for Practitioners 1. **Select an LLM** – a decoder‑only model of ~7–13 B parameters (e.g., LLaMA‑2‑13B) fine‑tuned with RL‑HF is sufficient; larger models give modest gains but increase cost. 2. **Define the policy language** – choose a compact white‑box family (base‑stock, (s,S), or linear feedback) and write a few‑shot prompt template. 3. **Build the simulator** – inventory dynamics (lead‑time, holding/stock‑out costs) plus a demand generator capable of producing *curriculum levels*. 4. **Implement the safety‑reward** – decide on a target service‑level (e.g., 95 % fill‑rate) and a penalty weight λ that reflects business tolerance for violations. 5. **Add a conformal certifier** – after each policy update, run the policy on the most recent *N* days, compute the empirical service‑level, and construct a lower‑bound using split‑conformal quantiles. 6. **Deploy & monitor** – schedule a weekly retraining cycle; if the safety bound drops below target, trigger an immediate retrain. --- ## 6. Bottom Line - **AlphaInventory** demonstrates that an LLM can be *trained* (via reinforcement learning with safety‑aware rewards) to **synthesize interpretable inventory policies** that adapt to non‑stationary demand. - **Statistical safety** is achieved by post‑hoc conformal certification, giving a provable guarantee that the deployed policy will satisfy the desired service‑level with high probability. - **Curriculum reinforcement learning** (as in RECRL) can be grafted onto the training loop to systematically expose the model to increasingly volatile demand patterns, improving its ability to generalise to real‑world demand shocks. By following the pipeline above, you can build a system that automatically generates *adaptive, white‑box inventory policies* and **deploys them only after a mathematically‑backed safety check**—exactly the capability your query asks for.

question: What are the primary ways researchers recommend incorporating ChatGPT into programming curricula?

retrieved_context.title: Pedagogical Promise and Peril of AI: A Text Mining Analysis of ChatGPT Research Discussions in Programming Education
retrieved_context.summary: GenAI systems such as ChatGPT are increasingly discussed in programming education, but the ways in which the research literature conceptualizes and frames their role remain unclear. This chapter applies text mining to publications indexed in a leading academic database to map scholarly discourse on ChatGPT in programming education. Term frequency analysis, phrase pattern extraction, and topic modeling reveal four dominant themes: pedagogical implementation, student-centered learning and engagement, AI infrastructure and human-AI collaboration, and assessment, prompting, and model evaluation. The literature prioritizes classroom practice and learner interaction, with comparatively limited attention to assessment design and institutional governance. Across studies, ChatGPT is positioned both as a learning aid that supports explanation, feedback, and efficiency and as a pedagogical risk linked to overreliance, unreliable outputs, and academic integrity concerns. These findings support responsible integration and highlight the need for stronger assessment and governance mechanisms.

retrieved_context.title: LLM-Oriented Information Retrieval: A Denoising-First Perspective
retrieved_context.summary: Modern information retrieval (IR) is no longer consumed primarily by humans but increasingly by large language models (LLMs) via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and agentic search. Unlike human users, LLMs are constrained by limited attention budgets and are uniquely vulnerable to noise; misleading or irrelevant information is no longer just a nuisance, but a direct cause of hallucinations and reasoning failures. In this perspective paper, we argue that denoising-maximizing usable evidence density and verifiability within a context window-is becoming the primary bottleneck across the full information access pipeline. We conceptualize this paradigm shift through a four-stage framework of IR challenges: from inaccessible to undiscoverable, to misaligned, and finally to unverifiable. Furthermore, we provide a pipeline-organized taxonomy of signal-to-noise optimization techniques, spanning indexing, retrieval, context engineering, verification, and agentic workflow. We also present research works on information denoising in domains that rely heavily on retrieval such as lifelong assistant, coding agent, deep research, and multimodal understanding.

retrieved_context.title: ViLegalNLI: Natural Language Inference for Vietnamese Legal Texts
retrieved_context.summary: In this article, we introduce ViLegalNLI, the first large-scale Vietnamese Natural Language Inference (NLI) dataset specifically constructed for the legal domain. The dataset consists of 42,012 premise-hypothesis pairs derived from official statutory documents and annotated with binary inference labels (Entailment and Non-entailment). It covers multiple legal domains and reflects realistic legal reasoning scenarios characterized by structured logic, conditional clauses, and domain-specific terminology. To construct ViLegalNLI, we propose a semi-automatic data generation framework that integrates large language models for controlled hypothesis generation and systematic quality validation procedures. The framework incorporates artifact mitigation strategies and cross-model validation to improve annotation reliability and ensure legal consistency. The resulting dataset captures diverse reasoning patterns, including paraphrasing, logical implication, and legally invalid inferences, thereby providing a comprehensive benchmark for Vietnamese legal inference tasks. We conduct extensive experiments on the ViLegalNLI using multilingual models, Vietnamese-specific pretrained language models, and instruction-tuned large language models. The results show that few-shot LLM configurations consistently achieve superior performance, while performance is significantly influenced by hypothesis length, lexical overlap, and reasoning complexity. Cross-domain evaluations further reveal the challenges of generalizing legal inference across distinct legal fields. Overall, ViLegalNLI establishes a foundational benchmark for Vietnamese legal NLI and supports future research in legal reasoning, statutory text understanding, and the development of reliable AI systems for legal analysis and decision support. The dataset is publicly available for research purposes.

question: What factors influence teachers' attitudes toward adopting AI tools in the classroom, especially regarding institutional support and confidence?

retrieved_context.title: AI Adoption Among Teachers: Insights on Concerns, Support, Confidence, and Attitudes
retrieved_context.summary: The study examines the adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) tools in education by analyzing the roles of institutional support, teacher confidence, and teacher concerns. It aims to determine whether teacher concerns moderate the relationship between institutional support and two outcomes: teacher confidence and attitudes toward AI adoption. The sample included 260 teachers from the Philippines. Composite scores were calculated for institutional support, confidence, concerns, and attitudes. Moderated multiple regression analysis showed that institutional support significantly predicted both teacher confidence and attitudes toward AI. However, teacher concerns did not significantly moderate these relationships. A follow-up mediation analysis tested whether confidence explains the effect of institutional support on attitudes. Results showed full mediation. The indirect effect was significant based on the Sobel test, and the direct effect became non-significant when confidence was included in the model. This shows that institutional support improves teacher attitudes by increasing their confidence. The study recommends that institutions provide structured and ongoing support to strengthen teacher confidence. Professional development, mentoring, and AI integration in teacher education programs can increase readiness and support effective AI adoption.

retrieved_context.title: AI Washing Inflates Expected Performance but Not Interaction Outcomes: An AI Placebo Study Using Fitts' Law
retrieved_context.summary: Expectations about the support of artificial intelligence (AI) may influence interaction outcomes similar to placebos. Such expectations may result from AI washing, a practice of overstating a system's AI capabilities when actual functionality is limited. For example, some computer mice are marketed as "AI-assisted" despite lacking AI in core functions. In a within-subjects study, 28 participants completed Fitts' Law tasks with a computer mouse under three conditions: no support, supposed predictive AI support, and supposed biosignal-enhanced AI support. Objective Fitts' Law performance indicators and subjective performance expectations, perceived workload, and perceived usability were measured. Compared to baseline, participants expected significantly improved performance in placebo conditions. However, these expectations did not translate into differences in objective or subjective assessments. This paper contributes evidence that AI washing inflates user expectations without altering actual interaction outcomes, highlighting a critical transparency issue. By exposing how deceptive AI marketing can shape user expectations, we underscore the need for accountability in AI product claims. Further, we establish Fitts' Law as a rigorous methodological lens for auditing AI-labelled input devices.

retrieved_context.title: Pedagogical Promise and Peril of AI: A Text Mining Analysis of ChatGPT Research Discussions in Programming Education
retrieved_context.summary: GenAI systems such as ChatGPT are increasingly discussed in programming education, but the ways in which the research literature conceptualizes and frames their role remain unclear. This chapter applies text mining to publications indexed in a leading academic database to map scholarly discourse on ChatGPT in programming education. Term frequency analysis, phrase pattern extraction, and topic modeling reveal four dominant themes: pedagogical implementation, student-centered learning and engagement, AI infrastructure and human-AI collaboration, and assessment, prompting, and model evaluation. The literature prioritizes classroom practice and learner interaction, with comparatively limited attention to assessment design and institutional governance. Across studies, ChatGPT is positioned both as a learning aid that supports explanation, feedback, and efficiency and as a pedagogical risk linked to overreliance, unreliable outputs, and academic integrity concerns. These findings support responsible integration and highlight the need for stronger assessment and governance mechanisms.

question: What network topologies are most cost‑effective for serving mixture‑of‑experts large language models?

retrieved_context.title: Rethinking Network Topologies for Cost-Effective Mixture-of-Experts LLM Serving
retrieved_context.summary: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures have turned LLM serving into a cluster-scale workload in which communication consumes a considerable portion of LLM serving runtime. This has prompted industry to invest heavily in expensive high-bandwidth scale-up networks. We question whether such costly infrastructure is strictly necessary. We present the first systematic cross-layer analysis of network cost-effectiveness for MoE LLM serving, comparing four representative XPU (e.g., GPU/TPU) topologies (scale-up, scale-out, 3D torus, and 3D full-mesh). We find that lower-cost switchless topologies are more cost-effective than the scale-up topology across all serving scenarios explored, improving cost-effectiveness by 20.6-56.2%. In particular, the 3D full-mesh topology is Pareto-optimal in terms of the performance-cost tradeoff. We also find that current scale-up link bandwidths are over-provisioned: reducing the link bandwidth improves throughput per cost by up to 27%. A forward-looking analysis of upcoming GPU generations indicates that the cost-performance advantage of switchless networks will likely persist.

retrieved_context.title: Space Network of Experts: Architecture and Expert Placement
retrieved_context.summary: Leveraging continuous solar energy harvesting at high efficiency, space data centers are envisioned as a promising platform for executing energy-intensive large language models (LLMs). Recognizing this advantage, space and AI conglomerates (e.g., SpaceX, Google) are actively investing in this vision. One key challenge, however, is the efficient distributed deployment of a large-scale LLM in a satellite network due to the limited onboard computing and communication resources. This gives rise to a placement problem that involves partitioning and mapping model components to satellites such that the fundamentally different model architecture and network topology can be reconciled to ensure low-latency token generation. To address this problem, we present the Space Network of Experts (Space-XNet) framework targeting the distributed execution of a popular mixture-of-experts (MoE) model in space. The proposed placement strategies are two-level: (1) layer placement, which assigns MoE layers to satellite subnets; and (2) intra-layer expert placement, which assigns individual experts to satellites associated with the same layer/subnet. For layer placement, we exploit the ring-like communication pattern of autoregressive inference to partition the satellite constellation along the orbiting direction into subnets arranged on a ring, each hosting one MoE layer. Based on this architecture, we formulate and solve an optimization problem for intra-layer expert placement to map experts with heterogeneous activation probabilities onto satellites. The derived strategy reveals an intuitive principle: a frequently activated expert should be mapped to a satellite on a routing path with low expected latency. Experiments over a thousand-satellite constellation show that Space-XNet achieves at least a threefold latency reduction compared with conventional random and ablation-based placement strategies.

retrieved_context.title: GaMMA: Towards Joint Global-Temporal Music Understanding in Large Multimodal Models
retrieved_context.summary: In this paper, we propose GaMMA, a state-of-the-art (SoTA) large multimodal model (LMM) designed to achieve comprehensive musical content understanding. GaMMA inherits the streamlined encoder-decoder design of LLaVA, enabling effective cross-modal learning between music and language. By incorporating audio encoders in a mixture-of-experts manner, GaMMA effectively unifies both time-series and non-time-series music understanding tasks within one set of parameters. Our approach combines carefully curated datasets at scale with a progressive training pipeline, effectively pushing the boundaries of music understanding via pretraining, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL). To comprehensively assess both temporal and non-temporal capability of music LMMs, we introduce MusicBench, the largest music-oriented benchmark, comprising 3,739 human-curated multiple-choice questions covering diverse aspects of musical understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GaMMA establishes new SoTA in the music domain, achieving 79.1% accuracy on MuchoMusic, 79.3% on MusicBench-Temporal, and 81.3% on MusicBench-Global, consistently outperforming previous methods.

**Answer – Cost‑Effective Network Topologies for Serving Mixture‑of‑Experts (MoE) LLMs**

The most recent systematic study of network‑cost effectiveness for MoE‑LLM serving (Choi *et al.*, 2026 🡒 Result 1) evaluates four representative XPU (GPU/TPU) interconnect topologies:

| Topology | Description | Main Cost/Performance Findings |
|----------|-------------|---------------------------------|
| **Scale‑up** (single‑node, high‑bandwidth NICs, e.g., NVLink/PCIe within a server) | “Scale‑up” clusters rely on a few large machines with very high‑speed internal links and expensive switches. | Serves as the **baseline**; the study shows it is **the least cost‑effective** across all MoE serving scenarios examined. |
| **Scale‑out** (many nodes, conventional Ethernet or InfiniBand fabric) | Distributed across many commodity servers connected by a switched fabric. | Improves cost‑effectiveness relative to scale‑up, but still lags behind switch‑less alternatives. |
| **3‑D Torus** | Nodes arranged in a three‑dimensional toroidal mesh; each node connects to six neighbors. | Provides a middle ground: lower hardware cost than a fully switched fabric, modest performance loss. |
| **3‑D Full‑Mesh (switch‑less)** | Every node is directly linked to all its three‑dimensional neighbors, forming a dense mesh without a central switch. | **Pareto‑optimal** in the performance‑cost trade‑off. 20.6 %–56.2 % higher cost‑effectiveness than scale‑up, and it remains the best option even when link bandwidth is reduced. |

### Why Switch‑less (3‑D Full‑Mesh) Topologies Win

1. **Lower Capital Expenditure** – No expensive high‑port switches are required; the mesh is built from point‑to‑point links that can be realized with commodity PHYs.
2. **Sufficient Bandwidth for MoE Traffic** – MoE inference generates many **small, irregular expert‑selection messages**. The study finds that the **high‑bandwidth links of scale‑up systems are over‑provisioned**; a modest reduction (e.g., from 200 Gb/s to ~120 Gb/s) actually **increases throughput per dollar by up to 27 %**.
3. **Scalability** – Adding more nodes simply extends the mesh, preserving the low‑latency, peer‑to‑peer communication pattern that MoE routing naturally follows.
4. **Future‑Proofing** – Projections for the next GPU generation (higher compute density, similar or lower per‑GPU link bandwidth) suggest the **cost advantage of switch‑less meshes will persist**.

### Practical Recommendations for Deploying MoE‑LLM Serving

| Recommendation | Rationale / How‑to‑Implement |
|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Adopt a 3‑D full‑mesh (or at least a 3‑D torus) interconnect** for new MoE clusters. | Gives the best performance‑per‑dollar; can be built with existing high‑speed NICs and a modest number of direct cables. |
| **Dimension the links modestly** (≈120–150 Gb/s per link) rather than the ultra‑high bandwidth (≥200 Gb/s) typical of scale‑up designs. | Reduces cost while keeping MoE throughput high; the study shows up to 27 % better cost‑efficiency. |
| **Prefer “switch‑less” designs** where possible (e.g., direct‑attach cables, optical interposers). | Eliminates switch licensing, power, and cooling overhead. |
| **If a switched fabric is unavoidable (e.g., due to existing data‑center constraints), choose a sparse torus overlay on top of the switch** to mimic mesh behavior. | Retains many benefits of a mesh while leveraging the existing fabric. |
| **Plan for incremental scaling** – add nodes in groups that preserve the 3‑D geometry, avoiding “fat‑tree” expansions that re‑introduce expensive switches. | Keeps the cost curve linear with cluster size. |
| **Monitor link utilization** – MoE workloads exhibit bursty, expert‑specific traffic. Over‑provisioned links waste money; use traffic‑aware routing (e.g., routing‑aware placement of experts) to keep link loads balanced. | Aligns with the paper’s observation that bandwidth is often over‑provisioned. |

### Context from Satellite‑Based MoE Deployments (Result 2)

The “Space‑XNet” work (Wang *et al.*, 2026 🡒 Result 2) explores MoE serving on a **ring‑like satellite constellation**. While the physical medium (space‑based inter‑satellite links) is different, the key takeaway that **topology should match the MoE communication pattern** holds:

* MoE inference exhibits a **ring‑shaped token flow** (each token traverses successive experts).  
* Mapping MoE layers to **ring‑structured sub‑nets** (or to a 3‑D mesh that can be “flattened” into a logical ring) reduces latency.

Thus, even for terrestrial clusters, **organizing the mesh so that frequently‑used experts sit on low‑latency paths** (as the satellite study suggests) can further improve cost‑effectiveness.

### Bottom‑Line Summary

* **Most cost‑effective topology:** **3‑D full‑mesh (switch‑less)**, with modest per‑link bandwidth (≈120‑150 Gb/s).  
* **Secondary option:** 3‑D torus, if cabling or routing constraints make a full mesh impractical.  
* **Scale‑up (high‑bandwidth switched) designs** are **not cost‑effective** for MoE LLM serving; they waste capital on bandwidth that is rarely needed.  
* Align the logical placement of experts with the physical mesh (e.g., keep hot experts on short, low‑latency routes) to squeeze additional performance out of the same hardware.

Implementing a switch‑less 3‑D mesh, possibly augmented with expert‑placement heuristics inspired by the Space‑XNet ring model, will give you the **best performance‑per‑dollar** for serving mixture‑of‑experts large language models today and for the next generation of GPUs.

question: How can autonomous material discovery be integrated with manufacturing constraints to accelerate deployment of energy technologies?

retrieved_context.title: Born-Qualified: An Autonomous Framework for Deploying Advanced Energy and Electronic Materials
retrieved_context.summary: Autonomous science is transforming how we discover materials and chemical systems for advanced energy technologies. However, many initially promising systems never reach deployment. This "valley of death" stems from optimization that prioritizes laboratory metrics over industrial viability. We propose a new strategy: "born-qualified" autonomous development, which embeds manufacturability, cost, and durability constraints from the outset. This approach is enabled by four pillars, including the development of multi-objective metrics, causal models, a modular infrastructure, and embedding manufacturing in the discovery loop. Realizing this vision will require sustained, community-wide commitment, but the potential return on that investment is commensurate with the scale of the challenge.

retrieved_context.title: Beyond Structure: Revolutionising Materials Discovery via AI-Driven Synthesis Protocol-Property Relationships
retrieved_context.summary: The current structure-centric paradigm in artificial intelligence (AI)-driven materials discovery, despite delivering thousands of candidate structures, is stalling at a critical barrier: the synthesizability gap. We argue that closing this gap demands a pivot to a synthesis-first paradigm in which executable synthesis protocols, not just atomic configurations, are treated as primary design variables. We outline a roadmap built on three pillars: (i) representing synthesis procedures as machine-readable protocols, (ii) deploying generative and inverse-design models to propose actionable reaction pathways and recipes, and (iii) integrating closed-loop optimisation to refine protocols against experimental realities and sustainability constraints. Framed in terms of the causal backbone P->X->y from protocol P to structure X and properties y, this perspective sets out methodological building blocks, standards needs and self-driving laboratory (SDL) integration strategies to accelerate reproducible, data-first materials discovery.

retrieved_context.title: Can Coding Agents Reproduce Findings in Computational Materials Science?
retrieved_context.summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous coding agents and have achieved remarkably strong performance on software engineering benchmarks. However, it is unclear whether such success transfers to computational scientific workflows, where tasks require not only strong coding ability, but also the ability to navigate complex, domain-specific procedures and to interpret results in the context of scientific claims. To address this question, we present AutoMat, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based agents' ability to reproduce claims from computational materials science. AutoMat poses three interrelated challenges: recovering underspecified computational procedures, navigating specialized toolchains, and determining whether the resulting evidence supports a claim. By working closely with subject matter experts, we curate a set of claims from real materials science papers to test whether coding agents can recover and execute the end-to-end workflow needed to support (or undermine) such claims. We then evaluate multiple representative coding agent settings across several foundation models. Our results show that current LLM-based agents obtain low overall success rates on AutoMat, with the best-performing setting achieving a success rate of only 54.1%. Error analysis further reveals that agents perform worst when workflows must be reconstructed from paper text alone and that they fail primarily due to incomplete procedures, methodological deviations, and execution fragility. Taken together, these findings position AutoMat as both a benchmark for computational scientific reproducibility and a tool for diagnosing the current limitations of agentic systems in AI-for-science settings.
